product.name: Social Post Helper
product.tagline: Metadata-first drafting for social posts, without AI.

hero.headline: Turn the clues already inside your media into a usable first draft.
hero.subhead: Social Post Helper reads dates, dimensions, device fields, GPS when available, and your own notes, then builds editable starting points for Instagram, Facebook, and YouTube without leaning on AI.

summary: Social Post Helper is a local-first web app that reduces the manual setup work around social posting by extracting media metadata, collecting a small amount of context, and generating deterministic drafts that stay editable.
audience: People preparing social posts from their own photos and videos
Makers sharing project progress or workshop updates
Travel or outing users who want a structured first pass before editing
Anyone who wants a metadata-first workflow before trying AI tools
coreProblems: Starting each social post from a blank field
Repeating the same date, location, and media context by hand
Keeping Instagram, Facebook, and YouTube copy aligned
Needing a lighter workflow than a cloud AI stack

features.title: Metadata-first intake
features.summary: Reads created date, GPS when present, camera or device fields, file type, resolution, and video duration when available.

features.title: Rule-based drafts
features.summary: Generates deterministic outputs for Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, hashtags, alt text, and short captions.

features.title: Manual context fields
features.summary: Uses category, what happened, audience, and extra notes to keep the drafts grounded in user intent.

features.title: Local persistence
features.summary: Stores preferences and the last session snapshot in browser localStorage for a local-first MVP.

features.title: Optional location handling
features.summary: Attempts reverse geocoding when enabled and falls back to coordinates when lookup is unavailable.

features.title: Editable export path
features.summary: Lets the user edit generated text in place, then copy or export it as JSON or plain text.

workflows.title: Upload and detect
workflows.summary: Bring in one or more photos and up to one video, then review the detected metadata summary before writing.

workflows.title: Set context
workflows.summary: Choose a category, add short notes, and set tone or platform preferences so the first draft matches the job.

workflows.title: Edit and export
workflows.summary: Use the generated outputs as a first pass, then adjust, copy, or export the drafts.

proofPoints.label: AI dependence
proofPoints.note: README explicitly says no LLMs, no cloud AI, no OCR, and no paid AI APIs.

proofPoints.label: Deployment surface
proofPoints.note: Documented in next.config.ts and the Pages workflow.

proofPoints.label: Known limits
proofPoints.note: No transcript extraction, OCR, direct posting, accounts, or restored file blobs in session replay.

faq.question: Is Social Post Helper using AI?
faq.answer: No. The documented MVP is deterministic and metadata-first, with future AI integration isolated for later work.

faq.question: What does it read from media files?
faq.answer: When available, it reads created date, GPS, camera or device fields, file type, image or video dimensions, and video duration.

faq.question: Where does the data live in the MVP?
faq.answer: Preferences and the last session snapshot are stored in browser localStorage.

faq.question: Can it restore the original uploads later?
faq.answer: No. The documented restore path brings back metadata context and drafts, not original file blobs.

faq.question: What is out of scope right now?
faq.answer: Direct posting, OAuth flows, scheduling, transcript extraction, OCR, user accounts, and cloud database features are all outside the current MVP.

catalog.title: Social Post Helper
catalog.blurb: A local-first web app that turns media metadata and manual context into editable social drafts without AI.

repoArchetypeReasoning: README describes a Next.js app with uploads, metadata extraction, editable draft generation, and local storage.
next.config.ts and the GitHub Pages workflow point to a browser-delivered product surface.
Folder structure and UI component names show an end-user web application rather than a library or internal service.
assumptions: Support and privacy URLs are placeholders for later central-site hookup.
No screenshots or logos were present in the repo, so the export uses original SVG artwork instead.
The public-facing category is treated as a web app because the documented delivery model is browser-based.
missingButUsefulAssets: A dedicated product icon or wordmark
A stable public product or repository URL if the central site should link out
